replies.author: @lasourisaboyante
replies.text:
​@ChristineNagel-e2efrankly I kind of made this transition myself several years ago. I used to work a very high-end job took me all over the world made a bunch of money.  I wasn't entirely a full with my money and I invested a significant amount of even though if I wasted a lot as well.

I came back to the United States to be closer to my parents and to help take care of them and some other family members that were ill.  And I had to take an entirely different route because the skills that I had weren't very valuable domestically.

So I went to work in a menial manual labor job for a year.  Loading building supplies for contractors into their trucks.  The great part about it is I dropped from 240 to 170 of pretty hard muscle.  Simultaneously I was learning very practical it skills, not coding, but installing cabling, fiber, switches, access points, sdwans that kind of thing.  An AI could very easily map out a network architecture, but it doesn't have the arms and legs to install it, now maybe within the next 50 years robots will be able to replace what I was doing, but I'll be long retired If Not Dead by then.

I built a nice little business over a couple of years and sold it and this time I wasn't a fool with my money.  I lived on mixed vegetables, ramen, and whatever meat was on sale for 5 years.  Invested at 80% of what I was making.  Made some decent calls with nvidia, tesla, amd, etc.  And as of a few months ago I have crossed the Rubicon of having enough money that I don't have to work ever again if I don't want to.

I'm still working on a help desk because that pays enough that I'm not touching any of my investments and it gives me insurance.  But I'm exploring a lot of the remote work opportunities and I have one job that I have which is being entitled entirely by an AI agent.  And my employer gives me top marks has no idea it's not me.

For young people what I suggest you do is learn something practical, not esoteric, and get started sooner rather than later.  We are still going to need a lot of people for construction, home construction, home improvement, electricians HVAC people Plumbing, and people to work on farms.

If that work is beneath you then I don't care what happens to you.

author: @BigDobsBeardBalm
text:
I worked at LinkedIn for 10 years, got laid off due to outsourcing and ai. During that time I built a beard care brand, hand crafted products - everything done by me. Worked my balls off for that company and my beard line. One paid off. 

I got connected to the barbering world through my product, Big Dobs Beard Balm. I decided to go to barber school after being given the boot. I've completed that program 2 weeks ago, am fully licensed, and own my barbershop. 

I'd never go back to the millennial corpo environment. 

Fun little fact - I sponsored one of Jeremy's MTG videos way back when. I still consider it a high point of that year of my business 🤘

replies.author: @TrueFork
replies.text:
I recently started to use AI extensively in coding and with it I can do in a week what would have taken a month or longer, but it doesn't do everything on its own, you have to know how to tell it what it needs to make. The eternal coding problem remains that the clients don't know what they want until after the system is delivered, but with AI it's much faster to produce prototypes and change them based on feedback. 
But this is nothing new, it's like when we switched from coding in assembler to higher level programming languages. At that time companies didn't keep doing the same with less people and smaller budgets, instead we did more, and made bigger, more amazing systems. I think the impact of AI will likely end up similar.

author: @robertedwards5159
text: Your end summary is 100% correct; I started working in tech 32 years ago and it was full of boring, sad, serious but highly competent and hardworking people who had to be at work on time (in the office - no remote/hybrid) and often worked late whilst having a manager constantly roaming around. Some companies had subsidised cafeterias onsite but you still had to pay so you would often bring a pack lunch or walk down to local supermarket for your lunch break treat. Maybe you would get lucky and have an onsite gym you could use within your hour lunch break (if you took it). Work was WORK NOT a social playground and your views/preferences stayed outside work even though at some companies we would often celebrate birthdays/anniversaries with a quick cake session. Also, it was very common for interns or newcomers to be fired due to not being competent enough even if they were trying - it was absolutely RUTHLESS with real tears being shed. Tech will go back to this as companies scrap fake imagery producing BS code/projects for lean, productive, competitive workforce who build real, reliable, cost effective products. Even with AI, high quality SERIOUS engineers/programmers will still be needed for the foreseeable future since humans are still responsible for differentiation/ innovation in products.
